g.data-narration: The Erdos-Renyi Random Network Model, denoted G(n,p), constructs a network of n nodes where each possible edge exists independently with probability p.
g.text: n = number of nodes, p = probability each edge exists independently

g.data-narration: Step 1: Initialize n isolated nodes. Here we place 6 nodes labeled A through F. No connections exist yet.
Step 2: For each possible node pair, generate a random number. If it is less than or equal to p, create the edge. Edges A-B, B-C, C-D, A-E, E-F, and D-F are formed randomly.

The degree distribution in a random network follows a Poisson distribution. Most nodes have a degree close to the average lambda equals p times n minus 1. The bell-shaped curve shows this concentration around the mean.

A critical phase transition occurs at lambda equals 1. Below this threshold, the network has only small disconnected components. Above it, a giant component emerges containing most nodes. When lambda exceeds ln(n), the entire network becomes connected.

Key properties of random networks: The clustering coefficient C equals p, which is very small for large networks. The average path length L scales as ln(n) over ln(lambda), giving the small-world property. The expected number of edges is p times n times n minus 1 over 2.
